title: Cutting clean curves
subtitle: Craft knife technique for smooth inside and outside curves in papercutting
excerpt: Smooth curves are the hardest part of papercutting with a craft knife. The knife wants to travel in a straight line; the paper wants to resist the change of direction. This technique covers the two types of curve, inside and outside, and the specific grip and paper-rotation approaches that produce clean, consistent results.
sourceNotes: Craft knife technique for papercutting synthesised from the broader paper arts tradition. No single attributable source; the inside/outside curve distinction and rotation method are standard practices documented across papercutting education and craft literature.

content.text: is not in the blade angle but in how you rotate the paper. For outside curves, the paper rotates into the knife. For inside curves, the paper rotates away from the knife. The blade stays in the same orientation; the work moves. Choosing the correct
for each curve determines whether you fight the blade or work with it.

attrs.body: Keep the blade angled low to the mat, cut away from fingers, and replace blades at the first sign of dragging.
attrs.title: Craft knife safety

content.text: Hold the knife like a pencil but lower: the handle at about 30 to 45 degrees from the mat surface, not upright. A low blade angle gives better control and keeps the cutting bevel in contact with the mat surface, which steadies the stroke. Grip lightly, a tight grip tires the hand quickly and reduces sensitivity. The blade moves primarily by pressure from the index finger, not by pulling from the whole arm.
Outside curves: rotating the paper into the blade

For an outside curve, a petal, an arch, the outside of a circle, place the blade at the start of the curve and make a short cutting stroke of 5 to 10 mm. Stop. Without lifting the blade, rotate the paper slightly in the direction of the curve with your free hand. Make another short stroke. Rotate again. Repeat in small increments around the arc. The blade stays roughly stationary in terms of direction; the paper rotates to produce the curve. The more gradual the rotation increments, the smoother the resulting curve.
Inside curves: rotating the paper away from the blade

Inside curves, the inside of a circle, the underside of a petal, a concave indentation, are harder because the blade wants to lift and skip. Use very short strokes: 3 to 5 mm at most. After each stroke, rotate the paper away from the blade slightly. Keep the strokes very controlled and do not attempt to cut an inside curve in a single long stroke. An alternative for tight inside curves: make a series of tiny straight cuts along the curve's edge, each angled slightly differently, and clean up any faceted edge with a single light finishing stroke.

Begin each cut at a corner or a straight section, not at the tightest point of a curve. Entering at the tightest point requires an immediate change of direction at the most demanding part of the stroke. Exiting a curve also tends to leave a small tail of torn paper; exiting at a corner or straight junction hides this tail naturally. Plan your cut path before putting blade to paper: identify the entry point, the direction of travel, and the exit point for each shape.

attrs.intro: Problems cutting curves with a craft knife.

items.fix: Make smaller rotation increments and shorter individual strokes. A smooth circle requires ten to twenty individual strokes, not four or five.
items.cause: The rotation increments between strokes are too large, producing a polygon rather than a curve.
items.symptom: The curve comes out as a series of facets or flat segments rather than a smooth arc

items.fix: Replace the blade immediately. If tearing persists with a fresh blade, try rotating the paper 90 degrees and cutting the same curve from the opposite direction; paper cuts more cleanly with the grain than against it.
items.cause: The blade is blunt, or the paper is being cut across its grain direction rather than with it.
items.symptom: The blade tears the paper along the curve rather than cutting it

attrs.heading: Common problems
